beginner.question: What is semantic HTML and why is it important for accessibility?
beginner.answer: Semantic HTML uses elements that describe their meaning (nav, article, form, table) rather than generic div/span. It's important because native elements have built-in accessibility features: keyboard support, ARIA semantics, and screen reader announcements.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <div> and <nav>?
beginner.answer: <nav> is a semantic element for navigation links. It creates a navigation landmark in the accessibility tree, so screen reader users can jump directly to navigation. <div> has no semantic meaning and provides no landmark.

beginner.question: Why should you use <button> instead of <div> with an onclick handler?
beginner.answer: <button> has native keyboard accessibility (focusable, activatable with Enter/Space), proper role semantics, and built-in form behavior. A <div> requires tabindex, role="button", keydown handlers, and still may not work with all assistive technologies.

beginner.question: What is the purpose of the <label> element?
beginner.answer: <label> associates descriptive text with a form control via the for/id attributes or by wrapping the input. Screen readers announce the label when the input receives focus, telling users what information to enter.

beginner.question: What is a proper heading hierarchy and why does it matter?
beginner.answer: A proper heading hierarchy starts with one h1, followed by h2 for sections, h3 for sub-sections, etc., without skipping levels. It matters because screen reader users navigate by headings to understand page structure.

beginner.question: What is the <main> element used for?
beginner.answer: <main> represents the dominant content of the page. There should be only one, and it should not be nested within other sectioning elements. It creates a 'main' landmark for screen reader navigation.

beginner.question: How do you make a form control accessible without ARIA?
beginner.answer: Use <label> with for/id to associate text with inputs, use <fieldset> and <legend> to group related controls, use appropriate input types (email, tel, number), and use <button> for submission.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <ol> and <ul> for accessibility?
beginner.answer: Both create list semantics, but <ol> (ordered list) indicates the order matters (steps, rankings) and screen readers announce the sequence number. <ul> (unordered list) is for items where order doesn't matter.

beginner.question: What is the <figure> element used for?
beginner.answer: <figure> represents self-contained content like images, code snippets, or diagrams, optionally with a <figcaption>. It can be moved away from the main content without affecting the document flow.

beginner.question: Why is using <h1>-<h6> better than styling <div> with CSS?
beginner.answer: Screen readers navigate by heading elements. A <div> styled to look like a heading is not a heading to assistive technology — it's just a <div> with text. Heading elements provide the semantic structure for navigation.

intermediate.question: How do you decide between <article> and <section>?
intermediate.answer: <article> is for self-contained, independently distributable content (blog post, news story, comment). <section> is for thematic grouping within a document. An article can contain sections, and a section can contain articles.

intermediate.question: What is the accessibility impact of using <details>/<summary>?
intermediate.answer: <details> with <summary> provides a native disclosure widget with built-in accessibility: Enter/Space to toggle, proper aria-expanded semantics, and role-based announcements. No JavaScript or ARIA needed for basic functionality.

intermediate.question: How do native HTML elements reduce ARIA dependency?
intermediate.answer: Native elements have implicit roles, states, and keyboard handling built in. Using <button> gives you role="button", keyboard activation, and form behavior for free. Using <select> gives you listbox semantics and arrow key navigation. This eliminates the need for ARIA attributes and custom JavaScript.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <blockquote> and <q>?
intermediate.answer: <blockquote> is for extended quotations (block-level) that may span multiple paragraphs. <q> is for short inline quotations. Both can use the cite attribute for the source URL. Screen readers may announce 'quote' around <q> content.

intermediate.question: How do you handle nested headings in complex layouts?
intermediate.answer: Nested sections create a logical heading structure. An <article> within a <section> can start its own heading hierarchy. However, the page-level heading outline should remain consistent. Use ARIA labels on sections to provide context without affecting heading hierarchy.

intermediate.question: What is the <dialog> element and how does it improve accessibility?
intermediate.answer: <dialog> is a native modal element with built-in focus management. showModal() opens a dialog with focus trapping, light dismiss (Escape), and proper dialog role semantics. <form method="dialog"> provides close behavior without JavaScript.

intermediate.question: How do semantic elements help with CSS and JavaScript?
intermediate.answer: Semantic elements provide CSS hooks with meaning (header, nav, main, footer) and JavaScript APIs (HTMLDialogElement, HTMLDetailsElement, HTMLProgressElement). They also enable progressive enhancement — semantic content works without CSS/JS.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <time> element?
intermediate.answer: <time> represents a specific period in time with a machine-readable datetime attribute. It helps assistive technologies announce dates consistently and enables browser features like calendar integration.

intermediate.question: How do you make a custom component accessible when no semantic HTML element fits?
intermediate.answer: First check if a combination of semantic elements works (e.g., <details>/<summary> for accordion). If not, use the closest semantic element and add ARIA roles. For example, use <button> with role="tab" for tabs — the button provides keyboard support, ARIA adds tab semantics.

intermediate.question:
What are the accessibility issues with using
for line breaks?
intermediate.answer:
only creates visual line breaks without paragraph semantics. Screen readers may not pause between
separated lines, making content read as one continuous paragraph. Use separate

elements for actual paragraphs.

advanced.question: How would you migrate a legacy div-based application to semantic HTML without breaking functionality?
advanced.answer: 1. Audit current structure and identify patterns. 2. Create a semantic wrapper with ARIA roles for backward compatibility. 3. Replace structural divs one section at a time (header, nav, main, footer). 4. Replace interactive divs with buttons/links. 5. Update CSS selectors from classes to elements. 6. Test each change with screen readers. 7. Remove redundant ARIA as native semantics take over.

advanced.question: What are the accessibility implications of using CSS display:grid or flexbox with non-semantic containers?
advanced.answer: CSS Grid and Flexbox are layout methods that work with semantic containers. The issue isn't the layout method but the container element. Using <div> containers for layout is fine as long as the content within uses semantic elements. The visual order (CSS order property) may differ from DOM order, which can confuse screen reader navigation.

advanced.question: How do shadow DOM and web components affect semantic HTML accessibility?
advanced.answer: Shadow DOM encapsulates semantics within components. Custom elements need to provide proper semantics internally. Use <slot> elements with fallback content, and ensure ARIA attributes penetrate shadow boundaries using ::slotted() or part attributes. Components should expose a clear accessibility interface.

advanced.question: What is the relationship between the HTML outline algorithm and heading hierarchy?
advanced.answer: The HTML outline algorithm (defined in HTML5) creates a document outline from sectioning elements (section, article, nav, aside) and headings. While browsers don't implement this outline visually, it theoretically allows each section to restart heading numbering. In practice, a flat heading hierarchy (h1-h6) is better supported by screen readers.

advanced.question: How do you handle accessibility when using frameworks like React or Vue that may strip semantic elements?
advanced.answer: Ensure framework components render semantic HTML, not nested divs. Use React Fragment or Vue template root instead of wrapper divs. Create wrapper components that accept 'as' props (as="nav", as="main" ). Avoid div soup — each component should output appropriate semantic elements.

advanced.question: What are the performance implications of semantic HTML versus div-based layouts?
advanced.answer: Semantic HTML can improve performance: browsers optimize native elements, less JavaScript is needed for interaction handling, reduced DOM size (no unnecessary wrapper divs), and better browser caching of semantic structures. Div-based layouts often require additional CSS/JS to achieve the same functionality.

advanced.question: How do you test if a page is properly using semantic HTML?
advanced.answer: 1. View the page with CSS disabled — does the structure make sense? 2. Use the HTML5 Outliner tool to check heading hierarchy. 3. Use accessibility inspector (Chrome/Firefox) to verify landmarks. 4. Run axe DevTools or Lighthouse. 5. Navigate with screen reader using only landmarks and headings. 6. Run the Nu HTML Checker for validation.

advanced.question: What is the correct way to mark up a complex data table with merged cells?
advanced.answer: For merged cells in data tables, use colspan and rowspan with multiple header references. Use the headers attribute on data cells to reference multiple header IDs. Use scope="rowgroup" and scope="colgroup" for group headers. Always include <caption> and use <thead>/<tbody>/<tfoot> for structure.

advanced.question: How does HTML5 semantic validation (Nu HTML Checker) help accessibility?
advanced.answer: The Nu HTML Checker validates HTML syntax and structure according to the HTML specification. It catches issues like missing required attributes (alt on images), incorrect nesting, deprecated elements, and structural problems that affect accessibility. Fixing validation errors often resolves accessibility issues before they're identified in manual testing.

advanced.question: What are the accessibility challenges with static site generators and markdown that produce non-semantic HTML?
advanced.answer: Markdown can produce non-optimal HTML: heading levels depend on markdown structure, images may lack alt text (if not specified), tables may be rendered as non-semantic HTML, and custom components may output nested divs. Solutions: use linting tools, enforce alt text in markdown linters, use semantic markdown extensions, and customize the HTML output templates.
